segments.text: giniwad normally be asking is what results are we seeing and what difference has this policy made.
That's because with legislation passed and funding approved you would reasonably expect
that government bodies would be getting on with implementation. You wouldn't normally be asking
why has the approved funding been spent on something else, why hasn't this policy been progressed
and why the outcomes as poor as they either were. Unfortunately that's what we haven't talked
to you about today. In 2017 the government launched an increasing deceased organ donation
and transplantation strategy, the details of which were on slide 6 of the packet we gave you.
The strategy was in response to New Zealand's low rate of organ donation, deceased organ
donation and transplantation that persisted over many years at the same time that other
countries were making significant revances. Enabling legislation to set up a national
body to lead the implementation of the strategy was passed by the parliament in 2019 with unanimous support.
New Zealand blood service was designated to be the national body to lead and drive change
and was renamed the blood and organ service. At the same time all parties in parliament expressed
great hope for the national body and hope that it would be well funded. Funding did not
materialise into a budget 2022 with funding to start on 1 July 2023. It was a small amount of money
and unlikely to do all what the strategy was required by the strategy but at least it was a start.
In late 2024 through a series of OAA requests to the blood and organ service
we established that almost all of the funding has been directed to supporting the functions
of organ donation New Zealand. ODNZ is the pre-existing national organ donation coordination body
which was transferred from Auckland DHB to the blood and organ service.
in 2020, only a small proportion of the funding has been used to regress the aims and actions outlined
in the 2017 strategy. We want to make it clear the section between organ donation New Zealand,
the national body to lead the strategy and the strategy itself. Organ donation New Zealand was
established in the early 2000s primarily to provide organ donation, ordination and
logistical services and is crucial for any transplant from a deceased donor to occur.
This strategy however is about new actions and activities that would lift the rate of deceased
organ donation and transplantation. ODNZ is a critical piece of the architecture
but implementing the strategy and the work of the national body is not about doing more of
what ODNZ was doing before 2020. This is a crucial distinction as some people will tell you that
activities of the organ donation New Zealand and the implementation of the 2017 strategy
are one and the same but they are not. They are quite different activities and functions.
The net result is that eight years after the strategy was launched, six years after
a neighbouring legislation was passed and three years after funding was provided,
very little has happened and New Zealand's deceased organ donation rate continues to drift
downward. We currently rank 32nd in the world for deceased organ donation rates.
With many people dying each year who could have had their lives extended had they received an
organ organ transplant. We have come to you as parliament as the national body
that oversees public expenditure and is able to hold an investigation of how we have fought
here. We are also able to recommend corrective action to get us back on track.
We are not here to throw the blood and organ service under the table and under the bus.
We don't really know what his
happen to have led to this outcome, but it is likely that the management and staff of the
Dunedin Organ Service have simply done the best that they could with the situation and the context
that they were given. The issues that have led to the situation probably lie deeper in the system.
As an indicator of this, the Ministry of Health's website currently describes the
Dunedin Organ Service's role and organisation as, quote, working to ensure the processes
for organ donation are nationally consistent and meet medical, ethical and legal standards,
unquote. Nothing about implementing the 2017 strategy or increasing deceased organ donation
rates. The Ministry of Health, the steward of the system, the author of the strategy,
seems to have given up on it. This is why we think a more in-depth investigation is required.
We have identified the things we think the committee may be able to do on slide four.
We ask that the select committee undertake an investigation because without it,
an issue that Parliament wants to progress and would reasonably have expected to be progressing
will simply get us again. Thank you, Ben Phillips.
Thank you. Thank you. Good luck. I thank you for your outline here. The strategy hasn't
been implemented and the funds that have been set aside for the NZ Fund Service
session haven't gone to where they're meant to go and we are tracking the wrong direction
when it comes to organ donation and having adverse health outcomes for New Zealanders
and it needs to be addressed and I think you've found pretty strong effort to see that.
Season action taken on that, Ken? Actually, Mr Chair, you just summed up
what I was going to say. And so, from this, you're looking for the committee to undertake an
inquiry or a briefing. Questions? Scrutiny questions, perhaps?
I didn't see any hands going out. I should have a question.
Could I put it to you that the major cause of this problem is the inability of other agencies
to get Health New Zealand to do anything? And is that what you mean when you say that
problems lie deeper within the system? I suspect there's a couple of drivers sitting here.
One answer I think that we have it done in different destinations, so we don't know.
So we are reading between the lines. I think the transfer from the Auckland DHB
to the Blood and Organ Service in November 2020 was problematic and probably insufficient
funding went wrong with the resources and the activities required.
We don't know whether that was sanctioned at the time by the oversight bodies at the time
that we have been in Ministry of Health. We were the diversion of funds to the existing
activities rather than through activities, increasing organ donation. We were that has
been sanctioned either by Health New Zealand or the Ministry of Health because at the moment
it's quite unclear who the oversight body for the Blood and Organ services
from where we sit in this system. So I think it probably stems from that, Dr Verrill,
probably a little bit earlier in time and possibly before Health New Zealand was established.
Can I just add to that? I think when we talk about deeper in the system, I think
organ donations are those issues that get lost in a big complex health system
that no one really owns. When you think about the patients who need transplants are coming from
many different specialties.

I think that's part of what we mean by saying
it's lying deeper in the system.

Is my focus on the strategy?
Are you aware of the members bill
that's in the besiketana at the moment
under the name of David McLeod
who is looking at the human tissue
Organda Nation Amendment Bill, looking at a national registry.
An Orban donor register?
Yes.
No, no.
To members, please.
One of the things I would say, though, is that the account system through the driver's license system
internationally has a very high sign-up rate compared to what happens elsewhere.
I would argue that one option to look at is to make sure that the driver's license system is actually able to be used effectively in the hospital and ICU session.
One of the things that we know about challenges for deceased donation in an ICU setting if a family reservations or concerns,
one of the things that really changes a family's view might be around about what did the person say on the Organda Nation Register.
a registered donor yes or no, access to the driver's licence system in an ICU setting is actually very difficult.
You can't access it in real time.
So, one of the first things that you would probably look at first is how do you make real-time access available in an ICU setting
so the information can be provided to family.
So, that helps them with the decision-making if they have reservations or concerns.
I'd probably look at streamlining that system and make it more effective than creating a new organ registry
which may not have the same sign-up rates and penetration rates as the driver's licence system does.
I was quickly summarising it may not.
Thank you. We're largely at time here.
I'm sure I saw you hit your hand up.
The link nurses, you know, a couple of these key recommendations are about
the management and ICUs of deceased people who go on to become deceased owners.
There's a lot of commentary we've got from the Button Organ Service about link nurses.
Could you explain the significance of them? Is that the right focus?
The link nurses are essentially kind of a coordination kind of link through from ICUs
into the logistics kind of the function and vice versa, as we understand it.
Funding was provided for those link nurses in the Budget 2020 decisions.
That funding, we understand, has been used for the purpose that it was intended and is continuing.
That's not the issue that we were raising with you today.
The rest of the funding that was announced in Budget 2020 was for the implementation of the strategy
and that has got lost in the system and has been used elsewhere.
The funding for the link nurses that was provided in Budget 2020 has been used for that purpose
and has continued to be used for that purpose and we have no concerns about that.
Subdumentary, so I'll be a surprise for you if the funding for link nurses has been absorbed into the general health...
New Zealand budget.
Yep, and we understand that it's being used.
So that's not your understanding?
No.
All right.
Hey, we're a few minutes over.
Thank you very much for your work and advocacy on the spot.
Thank you so much for your time.
Thanks.
Thank you very much.
That's very cool.
OK, and to follow on up to that, we can do the New Zealand Blood Service and
the New Zealand Blood Service.
Can you please turn your camera?
There we go.
Excellent.
We have Dr Sarah Morley, the Chief Medical Officer at the New
Zealand Blood Service.
Dr Morley, thank you for joining us.
We have you here for 10 minutes.
I'll let you speak to that.
If you want to provide us time for questions,
that would be great.
We appreciate it.
Thank you.
I hope that you've all had the opportunity to read the documents
that we sent, which I hope will have informed the committee
about some of the challenges that we have experienced
and also that we have been progressing a significant amount
of work towards the 2017 strategy.
Based on the recommendations that we have received from
that documentation, I wonder whether questions from the
committee would be the most helpful approach.
Absolutely.
Dr Vera.
Thank you, Dr Morley.
We just heard from petitioners requesting a select committee
investigation into this matter before.
And they were unaware that the committee would be
able to do so.
So, Dr Morley, thank you for joining us.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
And they were unaware that link team funding is no longer linked
local requirements and that you have no means to influence it and that funding is now at risk
according to your table under Strategic Priority 4. So, are you able to elaborate any further on
what's happened to that funding that was tagged for this important initiative?
Yes, I think so the funding certainly has been acknowledged as being
received by Health New Zealand. The challenge for individual intensive cares who are
effectively employing the linked nurse team and the linked doctor teams is that the funding
is no longer explicit in their budgets and I think that has resulted as a complexity of the
the budgetary changes within Health New Zealand and it leaves these post vulnerable at a local
level. We have sought several times to try to get clarity on it and we have just been assured that
the funding is there and that it must just be taken on trust that the linked nurses should
continue but I know that it continues to be a source of concern for intensive cares.
In point seven of your written submission you've got increased agency staffing enabled
ongoing development and completion of an electronic donor referral system in New Zealand.
This increases transparency, the accuracy of donation and transplantation referrals as
well as data collection. How is that development going and how widespread is this system?
So the system is in place internally within our team and it's providing data out in a more
consistent way to transplant surgeons in the first instance. That's phase one
unohani slapatrani.
We'd like to progress to the second phase,
but at this point we're just working through confirming
that the system is adequate for our needs,
and will work effectively.
The next phase would be to open up wider functionality
of the system to our transplant teams,
because one of the biggest challenges that we had historically
is drawing together large amounts of maniwalee collected information
to convey to the transplant surgeons while they make decisions about appropriateness of
transplantation. The final phase would be to support direct data entry from hospitals and from
referring teams into the system but we see that as being a pathway that will be best put in place
once we have the transplant services for requirements and activity across the system is able to be
improved. So really at the moment we're in phase one it's gone very well transplant teams are very
happy with the information that's coming through and it gives us a lot of security
around the consistency of information that transplant teams are seeing to make their
decisions and as I said in the the letter it is also improving our ability to accumulate data
to help us guide future improvements in care. Thank you Dr Warwick I apologies that this
sounds like a frivolous question but why have you not called just why is the NZ blood service not
called the NZ blood and organ service on any of your websites or even in your zoom date?
Yes no it's a it's a good question. The the challenge for us is that
New Zealand blood and organ service
is not the ideal framing for the bulk of the work that we do, which is the blood service work.
We need to maintain our own and existing brand, which is the New Zealand Blood Service to support blood and tissue donors.
We had contemplated considering a new name for organ donation New Zealand and the wider agency,
but also organ donation is a well established brand.
It's a challenge that others around the world have experienced.
So, for instance, Canadian Blood Services also runs organ donation.
They don't have one unifying brand.
They call that they have the same as we do New Zealand Blood Service or Canadian Blood Service
of the overarching brand and several others that underlie it to support the individual roles.
Okay.
Hey, I just wanted to ask, we've seen organ donations steadily declining since 2017.
What are we doing to reverse this?
Yeah, no, we had one year in 2017 where we had more donations than we do now.
Since organ donation has transferred to us
and since we've been able to staff and improve the service provided by the team,
we've had steady donations, but we have also been, as we explained in the letter,
through a period of significant challenge for organ donation around the world during COVID.
Post-COVID, we're also seeing, and again, this is an international reflection,
differences in approach and levels of trust in health
from families who may be donors.
I think also critically, we've also seen a considerable change in our own health system,
which we know is at an unprecedented, experiencing unprecedented levels of demand and challenge at the moment.
We know that that influences our ability to have conversations with families at the intensive care level as well.
Just going back to the link nurse funding and we've heard evidence prior to you coming on that the link nurses are there
and then we've heard from you that you are relying on trust on the service and that you have no influence over management of those teams.
Who is managing those teams and where is that, can you pinpoint where that oversight is coming from?
So in reality the work with the teams and the support of the work is led by our team.
However the funding that's applied in that space doesn't for instance enable adequate link nurse funding in any of the individual hospitals that have it
nor adequate doctor funding to support the work that we would like to see and that in fact many link nurses are doing.
Each of those nurses is employed by their individual hospital, their individual intensive care
and so the time they apply to their work really is managed locally.
We don't see at the moment an opportunity for overarching management of those teams and that for us is a challenge.
In other countries the link nurse teams are often funded directly through money coming from the National Organ Donor Agency
enabling them effectively to have a certain amount of management oversight of those roles.
We don't have that yet.
I'm going to Jenny because we are very short on time.
Thank you. I just want to talk about the assisted dying donation and the resourcing for that.
Is it additional resourcing that's required for assisted dying donation?
And where is the funding for it?
Can you just talk us through how much resource is required
and how much has been used since assisted design has come
and legally become available for people to choose?
So we're still, as you can imagine,
at a phase of really evolving and developing our policies
and work around assisted death and dying.
The effectively that work has all been done internally
by the Organ Donation New Zealand team
at developing the work liaising with the relevant bodies
at the Ministry of Health and in the hospital setting
and with the registrar.
We have established some themes that have been also reviewed
by the National Ethics Committee
and we are confident that we have a good pathway forward.
But all the work has been delivered by our team
by Organ Donation New Zealand as it stands.
It's a significant change in emphasis
and the nature of the work for them,
but they've stepped up in an incredible way
that I'm extremely proud of.
Thank you very much.
We're at time.
So thank you very much, Dr Molly, for coming in
and speaking to us and answering our questions here today.
Thank you for the opportunity.
We'll move back to the public.
